subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7, Year 4 mathematicians! In this unit you will explore the fascinating world of fractions. A fraction is simply a way of describing parts of a whole or parts of a group. You already meet fractions every single day without always noticing. When Amina shares an anjero equally between her three brothers, when Yusuf drinks half of his camel-milk cup, or when Halima measures three-quarters of a cup of flour to make sambusa dough at home in Hargeisa, they are all thinking in fractions.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why do fractions matter so much? Because the world is not always made of whole numbers. A trader at the Bakaara market rarely sells exactly one whole sack; he sells half a sack, or a quarter. A recipe rarely calls for a whole cup; it calls for two-thirds. Prayer times split the day into parts, and charity such as zakat is worked out as a fraction of what a family owns. When you become confident with fractions, you gain a tool you will use for the rest of your life – and you build the foundation you need for decimals, percentages and ratio in the years ahead.

As you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you, and if you can, some real objects to divide – a piece of paper to fold, some dates to share, or a drawing of a chocolate bar to shade. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Fold the paper, shade the parts, and say the fractions out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure – it is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next.
Recognise and write fractions as equal parts of a whole or a group.
Represent fractions with different models: area models, number lines and set models.
Read and write fractions in words and symbols (for example, 3/4 and ‘three-quarters’).
Compare and order fractions with the same denominator.
Compare and order fractions with the same numerator.
Add and subtract fractions with the same denominator.

concepts.title: What a Fraction Really Means
concepts.explanation: A fraction represents equal parts of a whole. The most important word in that sentence is equal – every part must be exactly the same size. If Farah divides a chocolate bar into 4 pieces but makes one piece huge and the others tiny, those are not quarters, because they are not equal. Fair sharing always means equal parts. Imagine we divide a chocolate bar into 4 equal pieces and take 3 of them. We have taken 3/4 (three-quarters) of the bar. Picture the bar as four equal squares in a row, with three of them shaded…
concepts.example: Every fraction is built from two numbers, one written above the other with a line between them:

concepts.title: Reading and Writing Fractions
concepts.explanation: Fractions can be written in symbols (like 3/4) or in words (like three-quarters). You need to move easily between the two. Here are the common fractions you will meet most often: So 5/8 is read ‘five-eighths’: the denominator 8 gives ‘eighth’, and because the numerator 5 is more than one we add an ‘s’. In the same way, 7/9 is ‘seven-ninths’ and 2/5 is ‘two-fifths’. Practise saying them aloud – hearing the words helps the meaning stick.
concepts.example: Fractions can be written in symbols (like 3/4) or in words (like three-quarters). You need to move easily between the two. Here are the common fractions you will meet most often:

concepts.title: Three Ways to Show a Fraction
concepts.explanation: One fraction can be pictured in several different ways. Being able to move between these pictures is a sign that you truly understand what the fraction means. There are three models you should master. An area model shows a fraction as part of a shape. The shape is divided into equal parts, and some parts are shaded. For example, a circle divided into 4 equal parts with 1 part shaded shows 1/4. A rectangle divided into 3 equal parts with 2 shaded shows 2/3. Area models are the pictures most people imagine first…
concepts.example: A length model shows a fraction on a number line. The distance from 0 to 1 is divided into equal steps, one step for each part named by the denominator. For example, on a number line from 0 to 1 divided into 5 equal…

concepts.title: Comparing Fractions with the Same Denominator
concepts.explanation: When two fractions have the same denominator, the pieces are all the same size, so comparing them is easy: the fraction with the larger numerator is larger. You are simply counting how many equal pieces each one has. For example, 3/5 is greater than 2/5 because 3 pieces is more than 2 pieces of the very same size (3 > 2). In the same way, 7/8 is greater than 3/8 because 7 > 3, and 1/4 is less than 3/4 because 1 < 3. We record these comparisons using three symbols:
concepts.example: So we can write 3/5 > 2/5 or 1/4 < 3/4. A helpful trick many learners use: the open, hungry mouth of the > or < symbol always points towards the bigger fraction, like a crocodile that wants the larger meal. When the…

concepts.title: Comparing Fractions with the Same Numerator
concepts.explanation: Comparing fractions with the same numerator works the opposite way, and this often surprises learners. When the numerators are the same, the fraction with the smaller denominator is larger. Why? Because the denominator tells you how many pieces the whole is cut into. When you cut a whole into more pieces, each piece becomes smaller. So thirds are bigger than fifths, and fifths are bigger than tenths. If you have one piece each way, one third is a bigger bite than one fifth.
concepts.example: For example, 2/3 is greater than 2/5, because thirds are larger than fifths (imagine cutting the same anjero into 3 pieces versus 5 pieces – the 3-piece cuts are bigger). In the same way, 3/4 is greater than 3/8,…

concepts.title: Ordering Fractions and Using a Number Line
concepts.explanation: To order fractions means to line them up, usually from smallest to largest. A number line is a wonderful tool for this. To place a fraction on a number line from 0 to 1, you divide the space between 0 and 1 into equal parts based on the denominator, then count along from 0 the number of parts given by the numerator. For example, to place 3/4 on a number line: divide the space from 0 to 1 into 4 equal parts, then count 3 parts from 0. Your mark lands three-quarters of the way along, close to 1. When you have…
concepts.example: If all your fractions share the same denominator, you do not even need to draw the line carefully; just order the numerators. To order 1/4, 3/4 and 2/4, look at the numerators 1, 3, 2 and put them in order: 1, 2, 3. So…

methods.title: How to compare two fractions
methods.example: A rectangle is divided into 6 equal parts and 5 of them are shaded. What fraction is shaded?
steps: Check whether the denominators are the same or the numerators are the same.
Same denominators: the larger numerator gives the larger fraction.
Same numerators: the smaller denominator gives the larger fraction.
Write the correct symbol: >, < or =.

methods.title: How to find an equivalent fraction
steps: Choose a number to multiply (or divide) by.
Multiply (or divide) the numerator by that number.
Do exactly the same to the denominator.
Check: the new fraction should cover the same amount.

methods.title: How to add or subtract fractions with the same denominator
steps: Check the denominators are the same.
Add or subtract the numerators.
Keep the denominator unchanged.
Simplify the answer if the top and bottom share a common factor.

methods.title: How to find a fraction of an amount
steps: Divide the amount by the denominator to find one part.
Multiply that part by the numerator (for a unit fraction, the numerator is 1).
The result is the fraction of the amount.

methods.title: Find equivalent fractions
steps: Solution: Multiply the top and bottom by the same number each time.
×3 gives 6/9.
×4 gives 8/12.

methods.title: Add fractions
steps: Solution: The denominators match, so add the numerators and keep the denominator: 2 + 3 = 5, so the answer is 5/6.
(5/6 cannot be simplified, so we leave it as it is.)
Check the add fractions result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Name the fraction
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle is divided into 6 equal parts and 5 of them are shaded. What fraction is shaded?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The whole is split into 6 equal parts, so the denominator is 6. Five parts are shaded, so the numerator is 5. The shaded fraction is 5/6, read ‘five-sixths’.

workedExamples.title: Write a fraction in words
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominator 5 gives ‘fifth’. The numerator 4 is more than one, so we add an ‘s’ to make it plural. The answer is four-fifths.

workedExamples.title: Compare (same denominator)
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominators are equal, so compare the numerators. Since 5 > 3, we have 5/8 > 3/8. The crocodile mouth opens towards 5/8.

workedExamples.title: Compare (same numerator)
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The numerators are equal, so compare the denominators. A smaller denominator makes bigger pieces, and 3 < 5, so thirds are bigger than fifths. Therefore 2/3 > 2/5.

workedExamples.title: Find equivalent fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply the top and bottom by the same number each time. ×2 gives (2×2)/(3×2) = 4/6. ×3 gives 6/9. ×4 gives 8/12. So three equivalent fractions are 4/6, 6/9 and 8/12.

workedExamples.title: Add fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominators match, so add the numerators and keep the denominator: 2 + 3 = 5, so the answer is 5/6. (5/6 cannot be simplified, so we leave it as it is.)

workedExamples.title: Subtract and simplify
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the numerators, keep the denominator: 7 − 3 = 4, giving 4/8. Now simplify: divide top and bottom by 4 to get 1/2. So 7/8 − 3/8 = 4/8 = 1/2.

workedExamples.title: Fraction of an amount
workedExamples.prompt: A shirt costs 24 shillings. There is a 1/3 discount. How much is the discount, and what is the sale price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The discount is 1/3 of 24, which is 24 ÷ 3 = 8 shillings. The sale price is 24 − 8 = 16 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Order three fractions
workedExamples.prompt: Put 1/2, 1/5 and 1/10 in order from smallest to largest.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The numerators are all 1, so the fraction with the largest denominator is the smallest. The denominators are 2, 5 and 10. Largest denominator first (smallest fraction): 1/10, 1/5, 1/2.

workedExamples.title: Two-step fraction of an amount
workedExamples.prompt: Farah has 15 dates and gives 2/5 of them to her little brother. How many dates does she give away?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by the denominator to find one fifth: 15 ÷ 5 = 3 dates. Then multiply by the numerator to find two fifths: 3 × 2 = 6 dates. So Farah gives away 6 dates and keeps 9.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Find: a) 1/4 of 20 b) 1/3 of 12 c) 1/2 of 3/4 cup.

Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: The Paper-Folding Fraction Kit
activities.materials: several sheets of paper, a pencil, and coloured pencils if you have them.
steps: Fold one sheet in half and open it. Shade one part. You have made 1/2.
Fold another sheet in half, then in half again. Open it: four equal parts, or quarters. Shade three to show 3/4.
Fold a third sheet into eighths. Shade parts to show 5/8.
Line up your halves, quarters and eighths. Can you see that 2/4 covers the same as 1/2, and 4/8 does too? You have discovered equivalent fractions.
Keep your folded sheets – they are a fraction wall you can use all through this unit.

activities.title: Fair Iftar Sharing (Set Models)
activities.materials: a small pile of dates, stones or bottle caps (about 12).
steps: Count out 12 dates. Share them equally between 3 people. What fraction does each person get? (1/3, and 1/3 of 12 = 4 dates.)
Now share the same 12 between 4 people. What fraction each? (1/4, and that is 3 dates each.)
Colour or set aside 3 of the 12 dates. What fraction have you set aside? (3/12, which simplifies to 1/4.)
Ask a family member to name a fraction of the pile, and you set aside that many.

activities.title: The Fraction Comparison Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards, each with a simple fraction written on it (for example 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 2/5, 3/8, 5/6).
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down.
Each player turns over one card.
Decide which fraction is greater. If the denominators match, compare numerators; if the numerators match, remember that the smaller denominator wins.
Draw a crocodile mouth (> or <) pointing at the greater fraction. The greater card wins the round.
This game trains you to compare fractions quickly and to explain your reasoning out loud.

activities.title: Build a Number Line
activities.materials: a long strip of paper, a ruler and a pencil.
steps: Draw a straight line and mark 0 at the left end and 1 at the right end.
Divide the space between 0 and 1 into 4 equal parts. Label the marks 1/4, 2/4, 3/4.
On a second line, divide 0 to 1 into 8 equal parts and label them in eighths.
Place the two lines one above the other. Which eighths line up with the quarters? (2/8 with 1/4, 4/8 with 2/4, 6/8 with 3/4.) You have found more equivalent fractions.

activities.title: Kitchen Fraction Investigation (Family Task)
activities.materials: a measuring cup and something safe to measure, such as rice, flour or water, with an adult helping.
steps: Measure out 1/2 a cup, then 1/4 of a cup. Pour the 1/4 into the 1/2 twice. What do you notice? (Two quarters fill one half: 2/4 = 1/2.)
A recipe needs 3/4 cup of flour. You have already added 1/4 cup. Measure how much more you need. (2/4 = 1/2 a cup more.)
Halve a recipe that asks for 3/4 cup of sugar. How much do you use? (1/2 of 3/4 = 3/8 cup.)
Talk about where else fractions appear in cooking.

activities.title: Design Your Own Fraction Story
steps: Write a short story problem that uses adding fractions with the same denominator.
Write another that uses subtracting fractions with the same denominator.
Write a third about finding a fraction of an amount of shillings.
Swap stories with a friend or family member and solve each other’s problems.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand fractions best? What was the trickiest part? Did folding paper help you see equivalent fractions? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: What a Fraction Means Numerator (top) = how many parts you have. Denominator (bottom) = how many equal parts the whole is split into. Example: 3/4 means 3 of 4 equal parts.

rules.title: Comparing Rule
rules.text: Same denominator: the bigger numerator is the bigger fraction. Same numerator: the smaller denominator is the bigger fraction. More pieces in the whole means each piece is smaller.

rules.title: Equivalent Fractions Rule
rules.text: Multiply or divide the top and bottom by the same number. 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8. They all cover the same amount.

rules.title: Add and Subtract Rule
rules.text: Same denominator: add or subtract the numerators only. Keep the denominator the same. Never add the bottoms. Simplify the answer if you can.

terms: A number for equal parts of a whole or group
The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Different fractions worth the same amount
A fraction reduced by dividing top and bottom
A fraction with numerator 1, such as 1/4
A line from 0 to 1 split into equal steps
commonMistakes: Parts must be equal in area
Divide top and bottom by 4 to get 1/2

realProblems.prompt: At the Bakaara market, Sahra buys a sack of rice and uses 2/6 of it in the first week and 3/6 in the second week. What fraction has she used, and what fraction is left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Sarah walked 5/8 of a mile to school. On the way she stopped at a friend’s house 2/8 of a mile from home. How much farther did she walk after leaving her friend’s house?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A shirt normally costs 24 shillings. With a 1/3 discount, how much is the discount and what is the sale price?
realProblems.answer: 1/3 of 24 = 8 shillings discount; sale price 24 − 8 = 16 shillings.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1/3 of 24 = 8 shillings discount; sale price 24 − 8 = 16 shillings.

realProblems.prompt: A class of 24 pupils is split into equal groups of 6 for a project. What fraction of the class is in each group?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra’s water jar was 4/5 full and Noor’s identical jar was 7/10 full. Whose jar had more water, and by how much? (Hint: 4/5 = 8/10.)
realProblems.answer: Zahra’s jar had more: 4/5 = 8/10 and 8/10 > 7/10, so 8/10 − 7/10 = 1/10 more.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Zahra’s jar had more: 4/5 = 8/10 and 8/10 > 7/10, so 8/10 − 7/10 = 1/10 more.

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Tom completed 5/12 of his homework before dinner and 4/12 after dinner. What fraction did he complete in total?
A carpenter has a 2-metre plank and uses 3/4 of it. How much wood does he use, and how much is left?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3/4 of 2 m = 1.5 m used, so 0.5 m left.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A blue ribbon is 3/4 metre long and a red ribbon is 3/5 metre long. Which is longer, and how do you know using same-numerator reasoning?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The blue ribbon is longer: 3/4 > 3/5 because with the same numerator, quarters are bigger than fifths.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Comparing Fractions with the Same Numerator.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Comparing fractions with the same numerator works the opposite way, and this often surprises learners. When the numerators are the same, the fraction with the smaller denominator is larger. Why? Because the denominator tells you how many pieces the whole is cut into. When you cut a whole into more pieces, each piece becomes smaller. So thirds are bigger than fifths, and fifths are bigger than tenths. If you have one piece each way, one third is a bigger bite than one fifth.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Ordering Fractions and Using a Number Line.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: To order fractions means to line them up, usually from smallest to largest. A number line is a wonderful tool for this. To place a fraction on a number line from 0 to 1, you divide the space between 0 and 1 into equal parts based on the denominator, then count along from 0 the number of parts given by the numerator. For example, to place 3/4 on a number line: divide the space from 0 to 1 into 4 equal parts, then count 3 parts from 0. Your mark lands three-quarters of the way along, close to 1. When you have…

questions.question: Work out 0.9 + 0.7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Add the tenths: 9 tenths + 7 tenths = 16 tenths = 1.6.

questions.question: Work out 0.1 + 0.4.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Add the tenths: 1 tenths + 4 tenths = 5 tenths = 0.5.

questions.question: What is 1/5 of 85?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Work out 0.3 + 0.2.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Add the tenths: 3 tenths + 2 tenths = 5 tenths = 0.5.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: What is 25% of 200?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Write 0.25 as a fraction.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: What is 25% of 150?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What does “Fraction” mean?
options: A number for equal parts of a whole or group
The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Different fractions worth the same amount
questions.answer: A number for equal parts of a whole or group
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Fraction means A number for equal parts of a whole or group.

questions.question: What does “Numerator” mean?
options: The top number – how many parts you have

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why do fractions matter so much? Because the world is not always made of whole numbers. A trader at the Bakaara market rarely sells exactly one whole sack; he sells half a sack, or a quarter. A recipe rarely calls for a whole cup; it calls for two-thirds. Prayer times split the day into parts, and charity such as zakat is worked out as a fraction of what a family owns. When you become confident with fractions, you gain a tool you will use for the rest of your life – and you build the foundation you need for decimals, percentages and ratio in the years ahead.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you, and if you can, some real objects to divide – a piece of paper to fold, some dates to share, or a drawing of a chocolate bar to shade. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Fold the paper, shade the parts, and say the fractions out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure – it is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next.
I can recognise and write fractions as equal parts of a whole or a group.
I can represent fractions with different models: area models, number lines and set models.
I can read and write fractions in words and symbols (for example, 3/4 and ‘three-quarters’).
I can compare and order fractions with the same denominator.
I can compare and order fractions with the same numerator.
